scenarios.opening_query: My mother's house sat empty for a year after she passed and now squatters have moved in.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Probate || Real Property > Litigation
scenarios.hidden_fact: Probate closed months ago and I hold the deed — evicting squatters from MY property is an ejectment matter, not estate work.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Probate's closed and the deed's in my name — it's about ejecting squatters from my own property now.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether title has passed making possession the issue
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The estate was never opened — the house is still titled to Mom, so nothing can be done until administration starts.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The estate was never opened — the house is still in her name.
scenarios.vetting_note: Title-passage fact again with an occupancy twist.

scenarios.opening_query: Dad's second wife emptied his safe deposit box the week he died and claims it was all 'gifts.'
scenarios.hidden_fact: The bank's sign-in log shows she accessed the box the day AFTER he died, using a POA that legally died with him — we want surcharge litigation and recovery.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The bank log shows she entered the day AFTER he died on a POA that died with him — that's litigation and recovery.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether documented post-death conversion supports suit
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The log shows the visits were months before he passed while she was his agent — that fight belongs in the estate's ordinary accounting.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The visits were months before he passed, while she was his agent — that's for the estate accounting.
scenarios.vetting_note: Bank-log timing decides suit vs administration.

scenarios.opening_query: I'm executor for my uncle and just found out he co-signed loans for half the family.
scenarios.hidden_fact: The estate's biggest asset IS those loans — three relatives owe it $95,000 on signed notes and refuse to pay; collecting for the estate is the work.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The loans are the estate's main asset — $95,000 owed on signed notes by three relatives who won't pay. Collection is the work.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the estate must collect debts owed TO it
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The co-signs make the estate the DEBTOR — lenders are filing claims, and sorting priority is normal administration.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The co-signs make the estate the debtor — lenders are filing claims into administration.
scenarios.vetting_note: Estate-as-creditor vs estate-as-debtor from the same discovery.

scenarios.opening_query: Our parents' trust says everything splits evenly but my brother the trustee has a different math.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Litigation || Wills & Trusts > Document Review
scenarios.hidden_fact: Before any fight, his lawyer sent a proposed 'family settlement agreement' — I need someone to review what signing it would waive, by Friday.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: His lawyer sent a proposed family settlement agreement — I need a review of what signing waives, by Friday.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a pending settlement document needs review first
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: No settlement offered — he's already distributed to himself 'per his interpretation,' and compelling an accounting is the move.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: No settlement — he already paid himself 'per his interpretation.' We need an accounting compelled.
scenarios.vetting_note: Document-pending posture precedes the litigation route.

scenarios.opening_query: Grandpa promised the lake cabin to me for years and now the family says the paperwork tells a different story.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Litigation || Real Property > General (Residential)
scenarios.hidden_fact: Grandpa's alive and sharp — the 'paperwork' is a deed he signed years ago to my aunt that may be invalid for lack of delivery; it's a title question between living people.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He's alive and sharp — the 'paperwork' is an old deed to my aunt that may be invalid for lack of delivery. It's a title question between living people.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the dispute concerns living-person title or a decedent's plan
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Grandpa passed in March — the codicil leaving the cabin to my aunt appeared during hospice, and contesting it is the case.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: He passed in March — the hospice-era codicil to my aunt is what we're contesting.

scenarios.opening_query: The funeral is Friday and the family is already fighting about who's in charge of everything.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Probate || Wills & Trusts > Document Review
scenarios.hidden_fact: Mom left a full binder — trust, pour-over will, directives — and what we actually need is someone to read it and tell us who does what; there may be nothing to fight about.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Mom left a full binder — trust, pour-over will, directives. We need someone to read it and tell us who does what.
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: There are no documents anywhere — no will, no trust — so intestate administration with a court-appointed administrator is ahead.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: There are no documents at all — it's intestate administration ahead.
scenarios.vetting_note: Documents-exist fact converts a fight into a reading.

scenarios.opening_query: My late husband's ex-wife says half his pension survivor benefit belongs to her.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Wills & Trusts > Probate || Family Law > QDROs
scenarios.hidden_fact: Their divorce decree awarded her half via a QDRO that was never implemented — resolving that order against the plan is where this gets decided, not the estate.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Their decree awarded her half through a QDRO nobody ever implemented — that order against the plan is where it gets decided.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether an unimplemented QDRO governs the benefit
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: Their decree waived all pension claims — her demand has no order behind it, and the estate just needs to administer the benefit to me.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Their decree waived pension claims — there's no order behind her demand.
scenarios.vetting_note: Decree-content fact routes between plan-order practice and administration.

scenarios.opening_query: Mom added my brother to her bank account 'for convenience' and now that she's gone he says it's all his.
scenarios.hidden_fact: The bank's signature card shows 'joint with survivorship' — but Mom's contemporaneous letter to her lawyer says convenience only; overcoming the survivorship presumption is a court fight.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The signature card says survivorship — but Mom's own letter to her lawyer says convenience only. Overcoming that presumption is a court fight.
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The card actually shows no survivorship — 'convenience signer' only — so the account simply belongs to the estate to administer.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The card shows convenience signer, no survivorship — the account just belongs to the estate.
scenarios.vetting_note: Signature-card content is the checkable routing fact.
